title: Timelines at Scale
author: Raffi Krikorian (Twitter)
note: First-hand account of Twitter's fan-out architecture and materialized home timelines, the exact case study the chapter opens with, including the read/write asymmetry numbers.

title: The Tail at Scale
author: Jeffrey Dean and Luiz André Barroso (Google)
note: The canonical paper on tail latency amplification: why p99 dominates user experience when one request fans out to many backends, plus tail-tolerant techniques like hedged requests.

title: How NOT to Measure Latency
author: Gil Tene (Azul Systems)
note: Explains coordinated omission and why averages and naive percentile math mislead, motivating HdrHistogram and correct response-time (vs service-time) measurement under queueing.

title: Service Level Objectives (Site Reliability Engineering, Chapter 4)
author: Chris Jones, John Wilkes, and Niall Murphy (Google)
note: Defines SLIs, SLOs, and SLAs with concrete guidance on choosing percentile-based targets, directly grounding the chapter's discussion of performance promises.

title: Exponential Backoff and Jitter
author: Marc Brooker (AWS)
note: Shows with simulations why retrying clients cause contention spikes and how adding jitter to exponential backoff tames retry storms, a key defense against metastable failures.

title: Blameless PostMortems and a Just Culture
author: John Allspaw (Etsy)
note: The foundational essay on blameless postmortems: why investigating the situational causes of failure instead of punishing operators makes systems more reliable.

title: Metastable Failures in Distributed Systems
author: Nathan Bronson, Abutalib Aghayev, Aleksey Charapko, and Timothy Zhu
note: Names and dissects the failure mode where a system stays overloaded after the trigger is gone (e.g. retry storms), giving readers a vocabulary for the chapter's hardest reliability concept.

title: Principles of Chaos Engineering
author: Chaos Engineering community (originating at Netflix)
note: Concise manifesto on deliberately injecting real-world faults as controlled experiments to verify fault tolerance, formalizing the practice the chapter attributes to Netflix's Chaos Monkey.
